problem_summary: 2016 MCM Problem A ("Have a Hot Bath"): a person in a bathtub of cooling water adds a constant trickle of hot water to keep the bath temperature even throughout the tub and as close as possible to the initial comfortable temperature, while wasting as little water as possible. The team must build a space-and-time model of the water temperature, recommend a best add-water strategy, analyze sensitivity to tub shape/volume, person shape/volume/temperature and motion, and the effect of an initial bubble additive, plus a one-page non-technical explanation for users.
models_used: Heat-transfer PDE model: a 3D-in-space, 1D-in-time ("four-dimensional") transient heat-conduction equation for water temperature T(x,y,z,t), derived from Fourier's law plus an energy-conservation integral balance, with an added volumetric source term f = (T_r - T_c)V_p / V_Omega representing the hot-water trickle modeled as a point heat source at the surface

Robin (third-kind/convective) boundary conditions on the three interfaces: water-air (Gamma_1: lambda_1 T + lambda dT/dn_1 = lambda_1 T_1), water-tub wall (Gamma_2: insulated/Neumann dT/dn_2 = 0), and water-person (Gamma_3: lambda_3 T + lambda dT/dn_3 = lambda_3 T_3); person geometry idealized as two stacked cuboids
Finite Element Analysis numerical solution of the PDE in ANSYS to obtain the transient 2D/3D temperature distribution over the 30-min bath

Multi-objective optimization model with design variables T_r (inflow temperature) and V_p (inflow volume per unit time); three objectives: minimize final-temperature variance sigma^2 (evenness), minimize |Delta T_z| = T_z_bar - T_0 (closeness to initial temp), minimize V_p (water waste), subject to box constraints 317K<=T_r<=323K and 0<=V_p<=2e-4 m^3
Weighted-sum scalarization converting the 3 objectives into a single objective min(w1*sigma^2 + w2*Delta T_z + w3*V_p) with weights set to w1=0.6, w2=0.4, w3=0
Genetic Algorithm (MATLAB calling ANSYS in the loop) to solve the scalarized optimization; GA parameters: 8 generations, crossover prob 0.4, mutation prob 0.2, population/sample size 41
key_techniques: Deriving the governing PDE from first principles: starts from an energy-conservation integral over an arbitrary time window [t1,t2] and volume Omega, then uses the arbitrariness of the time interval to strip the integral and recover the pointwise heat equation, rather than just quoting it

Modeling the faucet inflow not as a moving-fluid (convection) problem but as a volumetric heat source term f added to the conduction equation, deliberately assuming a 'trickle' so convection/flow can be neglected and the math stays a pure conduction PDE

Encoding each physical interface as a distinct boundary condition (Robin for air and person, Neumann/insulated for the ceramic tub) so that every sensitivity scenario maps cleanly onto changing one coefficient (lambda_1, lambda_3)

Reusing the SAME validated PDE+FEA forward model inside the optimization loop, and making the entire sensitivity analysis (tub area/height/shape, person volume/shape/temperature, motion, bubble additive) reduce to perturbing a single parameter and re-running the coupled MATLAB-ANSYS-GA pipeline

Representing complex geometry (person, trapezoidal-cross-section tub) as simple primitives (two cuboids, two cylinders, isosceles-trapezoid prism) to keep the FEA meshing tractable while still capturing surface-area effects

Translating quantitative optima into plain-language, actionable rules of thumb for the user one-pager (e.g., 'increase inflow temperature by ~14.28K per extra cubic meter of tub', '+0.5K if you are larger', '+2K for a child who plays vigorously')
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on three counts. First, it answers the literal prompt completely and in order: a genuine space-AND-time temperature model (a real transient PDE, not a lumped ODE), an explicit best strategy with numbers, every required sensitivity factor addressed (tub shape/volume, person shape/volume/temperature, motion, bubble additive), AND the often-skipped one-page user explanation. Second, it couples two non-trivial methods into one coherent pipeline: a physics-based FEA forward model feeding a GA-driven multi-objective optimizer, with MATLAB orchestrating ANSYS in the loop — judges reward a model that actually closes the loop from physics to decision. Third, the derivation is rigorous and self-contained (Fourier's law -> integral energy balance -> pointwise PDE -> three boundary conditions + initial condition), so the modeling reads as principled rather than hand-waved. The sensitivity section is broad and each result is given a physical explanation (more surface area -> more heat loss -> hotter/larger inflow), demonstrating the model is being interpreted, not just run.
section_highlights: Structure tracks the prompt and the modeling pipeline cleanly: Introduction/Background -> Problem restatement -> Terminology + Symbols table -> numbered General Assumptions -> Heat Transfer Model (built incrementally: basic no-person model, then 'take people into consideration' adds Gamma_3, then ANSYS simulation) -> Multi-objective optimization (objectives, constraints, weighted scalarization, GA steps) -> a wide Sensitivity Analysis with one subsection per required factor -> Strengths/Weaknesses -> the user-facing one-pager -> References. Strong writing moves: every equation is followed by a where-block defining each symbol; assumptions are stated up front and then explicitly invoked later (e.g., insulated ceramic wall justifies the Neumann BC); results are reported as concrete tables (best strategy: T_r=322.71K, V_p=1.2e-4 m^3/s, sigma^2=0.05513, Delta T_z=-0.125K, waste=0.23 m^3) with accompanying ANSYS heat-distribution figures; each sensitivity result is paired with a physical mechanism rather than left as a bare number. Incremental model-building (add one boundary condition at a time) makes the complexity feel earned and easy to follow.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Physically questionable neglect of convection: real bathtub reheating is dominated by buoyancy-driven natural convection and mixing, yet the model assumes pure conduction in still water (justified only by the 'trickle' assumption). Pure conduction in water would in reality leave huge thermal gradients — the model likely understates how the bath actually equilibrates

Reported final-temperature variance/distribution is internally inconsistent and the units are sloppy: the summary reports a variance of 20.05513K in one place but Table 4 gives sigma^2=0.05513; the source term f is listed as '100J' (energy, not a rate/temperature-rate), and 'water wasted 30.23m' is missing units — judges tolerate it but it is a real rigor gap

Genetic Algorithm is under-powered and under-justified: only 8 generations and population 41 for a 2-variable box-constrained problem is far too few to trust as a global optimum, and a 2D continuous problem this smooth arguably did not need a GA at all (grid search or gradient methods would suffice); GA parameter choices are unexplained

Weighted-sum scalarization with w3=0 silently drops the water-waste objective entirely, so the 'multi-objective' optimization effectively optimizes only evenness and closeness; weights 0.6/0.4 are asserted with no Pareto-front exploration or justification

Insulated-wall (Neumann) assumption for the tub plus a constant room temperature ignores the dominant real-world heat loss paths (evaporation from the surface, conduction/radiation through a non-ideal tub), which the team itself flags as a weakness

Several figures appear garbled/auto-extracted (heatmap tables with nan, inconsistent ANSYS legend values), and the person is only ever idealized as cuboids/cylinders — the team admits non-cuboid baselines were not explored

T_c (outlet temperature) and the relationship between f and the BCs are introduced but the closed loop between the average-temperature objective and the time-dependent solution is lightly handled; evaporative latent-heat loss, the single biggest term in real bath cooling, is entirely absent
reusable_patterns: Forward-model + optimizer sandwich: build and validate a physics simulation (PDE/FEA) first, then wrap an optimizer (GA/scalarized multi-objective) around it that calls the simulation as a black box — a template for any 'design the best control/strategy' MCM problem

Derive-don't-quote the governing equation: open from a conservation law as an integral over an arbitrary region/time window and collapse it to the pointwise PDE, which signals rigor to judges and makes assumptions explicit

One-boundary-condition-per-interface design so that the entire sensitivity analysis becomes 'change exactly one coefficient and re-run' — gives a clean, exhaustive, easy-to-narrate sensitivity section

Geometry-as-primitives: approximate awkward real shapes (a human body, a contoured tub) as stacked cuboids/cylinders/trapezoidal prisms to keep meshing and the model tractable while still capturing the first-order driver (surface area)

Always-pair-number-with-mechanism: after every sensitivity result, state the physical reason for the trend (surface area up -> heat loss up -> hotter inflow needed); converts a table dump into demonstrated understanding
Symbol table + per-equation where-blocks + numbered assumptions as a writing scaffold that keeps a heavily mathematical paper readable
Take the prompt's deliverables literally and in order, including the non-technical one-pager, and translate optima into concrete rules of thumb the lay reader can act on
Build the model incrementally in the text (basic case -> add the person -> add motion/additive), so each layer of complexity is motivated and the reader is never shown the full system at once
